cards.prompt: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit.
cards.explanation: CPU is short for Central Processing Unit, the main processor of a computer.

cards.prompt: 'www' stands for World Wide Web.
cards.explanation: www stands for World Wide Web, the system of web pages on the internet.

cards.prompt: Email addresses usually contain the '@' symbol.
cards.explanation: An email address places the '@' symbol between the user name and the domain.

cards.prompt: Wi-Fi lets devices connect to the internet without cables.
cards.explanation: Wi-Fi uses radio signals to connect devices to a network without wires.

cards.prompt: 1 gigabyte (GB) is larger than 1 megabyte (MB).
cards.explanation: 1 GB is about 1,000 MB, so it is much larger than 1 MB.

cards.prompt: Android and iOS are operating systems for smartphones.
cards.explanation: Android (Google) and iOS (Apple) are the two most widely used smartphone operating systems.

cards.prompt: RAM keeps all its data even after the computer is turned off.
cards.explanation: RAM is volatile memory; its contents are lost when the power is off.

cards.prompt: HTML is an antivirus program.
cards.explanation: HTML is a markup language used to build web pages, not an antivirus program.

cards.prompt: One byte is smaller than one bit.
cards.explanation: One byte equals eight bits, so a byte is larger than a bit.

cards.prompt: iOS is an operating system made by Google.
cards.explanation: iOS is made by Apple; Google makes Android.

cards.prompt: A 'URL' is a type of computer virus.
cards.explanation: A URL is a web address that points to a page or file, not a virus.

cards.prompt: The internet was invented in the year 1500.
cards.explanation: The internet grew out of networks developed in the 20th century, not in 1500.

cards.prompt: A mouse and a keyboard are input devices.
cards.explanation: Input devices like the mouse and keyboard send information from the user into the computer.

cards.prompt: A computer virus is a biological illness that can infect people.
cards.explanation: A computer virus is malicious software that spreads between computers; it cannot infect people.

cards.prompt: Google is a widely used internet search engine.
cards.explanation: Google is the world's most widely used search engine for finding information on the web.

cards.prompt: A firewall is a physical wall built to keep computers warm.
cards.explanation: A firewall is security software or hardware that blocks unauthorized network traffic.

cards.prompt: PDF is a common file format for documents.
cards.explanation: PDF (Portable Document Format) is widely used to share documents that look the same on any device.

cards.prompt: Cloud storage keeps your files in actual clouds in the sky.
cards.explanation: Cloud storage saves files on remote servers in data centers, accessed over the internet.

cards.prompt: Bluetooth connects devices wirelessly over short distances.
cards.explanation: Bluetooth uses short-range radio waves to link devices like headphones and phones without cables.

cards.prompt: Deleting a file always destroys it instantly beyond any recovery.
cards.explanation: Deleted files often go to a recycle bin first, and even afterwards they can sometimes be recovered with special tools.

cards.prompt: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus.
cards.explanation: USB (Universal Serial Bus) is a standard for connecting devices and transferring data and power.

cards.prompt: Private (incognito) browsing makes you completely anonymous on the internet.
cards.explanation: Incognito mode only stops the browser from saving your local history; websites and network providers can still see your activity.

cards.prompt: A web browser is a program used to visit websites.
cards.explanation: Browsers such as Chrome, Safari, and Firefox display web pages and let you navigate the internet.

cards.prompt: A megapixel measures how loud a speaker can be.
cards.explanation: A megapixel is one million pixels and measures image resolution, not loudness.

cards.prompt: Computers ultimately store data as ones and zeros.
cards.explanation: All digital data is stored in binary form, using only the digits 1 and 0.

cards.prompt: Linux is a type of computer hardware.
cards.explanation: Linux is an operating system, like Windows or macOS, not a piece of hardware.

cards.prompt: An SSD has no spinning parts, unlike a traditional hard disk drive.
cards.explanation: An SSD stores data on flash memory chips with no moving parts, which makes it faster and more durable than a spinning hard disk.

cards.prompt: Bluetooth and Wi-Fi are exactly the same technology.
cards.explanation: They are different wireless standards: Bluetooth links nearby devices, while Wi-Fi connects devices to a network over longer ranges.

cards.prompt: The 'CC' field in an email sends a copy of the message to additional recipients.
cards.explanation: CC stands for 'carbon copy'; everyone in the CC field receives a copy of the email.

cards.prompt: A 'bug' in software means an insect is trapped inside the computer.
cards.explanation: A software bug is an error in a program's code; the term was popularized after a moth was found in an early computer.

cards.prompt: A screenshot is an image of what is currently shown on a screen.
cards.explanation: Taking a screenshot captures the current contents of the display as an image file.

cards.prompt: The 'HTTP' at the start of a web address stands for 'High Tech Transfer Protocol'.
cards.explanation: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol, the protocol used to load web pages.

cards.prompt: A QR code can be scanned with a smartphone camera.
cards.explanation: A QR code is a square barcode that smartphone cameras can scan to open links or read information.

cards.prompt: 'AI' stands for 'Automatic Internet'.
cards.explanation: AI stands for Artificial Intelligence, technology that lets computers perform tasks that normally require human intelligence.

cards.prompt: A strong password helps protect an account from unauthorized access.
cards.explanation: Passwords verify a user's identity; long, unique passwords make accounts much harder to break into.

cards.prompt: The first electronic computers were small enough to fit in a pocket.
cards.explanation: Early computers like ENIAC filled entire rooms and weighed many tons; pocket-sized computing came decades later.
